trivia_bullets.text: Bleeding Woods is part of the "unlucky lands" cycle, a full set of ten new common dual lands introduced in Duskmourn: House of Horror (released 2024-09-27). The cycle's shared mechanic — entering untapped only when a player has 13 or less life — is a direct reference to triskaidekaphobia, the fear of the number 13, woven throughout the set's horror theme. The name of the cycle among players and vendors is "unlucky lands." [CoolStuffInc preview article by Paige Smith, 2024-09-02; Unlucky land — MTG Wiki, via WebSearch snippet]
sources: CoolStuffInc: Ten New Common Dual Lands in Duskmourn, Paige Smith (2024-09-02)
Unlucky land — MTG Wiki, via WebSearch snippet

trivia_bullets.text: Bleeding Woods produces red or green mana and is legal in Standard, Pioneer, Modern, Legacy, Vintage, Pauper, and Commander as of its release date [Scryfall legalities]. Its EDHREC rank of 9521 places it in the lower-middle range of Commander utility compared to its cycle mate Etched Cornfield (rank 10669), suggesting modest Commander adoption — the 13-life trigger is rarely relevant in Commander's 40-life format. [Scryfall; EDHREC via Scryfall rank field]
sources: EDHREC rank via Scryfall API response

trivia_bullets.text: Flavor text: "They say if someone lies down to sleep beneath the trees, in the morning nothing will remain but a few scattered bones and a bed of blood-red lilies." The flavor describes the forest as predatory — the land itself consuming anyone who rests in it. This fits the Duskmourn plane's design premise: even the natural terrain is subsumed into the House's appetite. [Scryfall]

Duskmourn was designed as Magic's first top-down modern horror set, focused on 1970s-1980s horror media rather than the Gothic horror of Innistrad. Vision design was led by Annie Sardelis (internal codename "Swimming"); the set's creative premise — a single haunted house as an entire plane — dictated that even outdoor environments like forests and fields would bear the visual language of the House (floating windows, architectural frames embedded in organic surfaces). [Wizards of the Coast: "Duskmourn: House of Horror Vision Design Handoff, Part 1," magic.wizards.com]
sources: Wizards of the Coast: Duskmourn Vision Design Handoff Part 1, magic.wizards.com
